text.title: Customer Journey Mapping — Examples and Common Mistakes
text.summary: E-commerce purchase journey example with stage-by-stage breakdown and key insight generation.

example.note: Stages: Discover → Research → Decide → Purchase → Receive → Use. Discover: user searches, sees ad; emotion Curious; pain "hard to find what I need." Research: compares options; emotion Engaged; pain "too many choices." Decide: reads reviews; emotion Uncertain; pain "conflicting reviews." Purchase: checkout; emotion Anxious; pain "long form, hidden fees." Receive: tracks order; emotion Impatient; pain "no tracking updates." Use: unboxes; emotion varies; pain "not as expected." Key insight: Checkout produces the highest anxiety and has the most friction — simplifying checkout is the highest-leverage opportunity.

section.title: Stage Metrics by Journey Phase
section.p: Awareness: reach, impressions. Consideration: engagement, time on site. Purchase: conversion rate. Onboarding: activation rate. Usage: retention, engagement. Support: resolution time, CSAT. Loyalty: NPS, repeat purchase rate.

rule.statement: Attach at least one measurable metric to each stage so improvements can be tracked quantitatively after implementation.
rule.rationale: Journey maps without metrics remain a soft deliverable. Metrics convert the map from a qualitative description into a measurement framework that tracks change over time.

antipattern.description: Creating a journey map from team assumptions rather than user research.
antipattern.reason: Teams consistently underestimate user anxiety at handoff points and overestimate satisfaction at product touchpoints. Assumption maps reinforce these biases rather than correcting them.

antipattern.description: Mapping actions only — no emotions or thoughts row in the map.
antipattern.reason: Actions without emotional context miss the moments where users are most at risk of abandonment or error. The emotion curve is where prioritization decisions come from.

antipattern.description: Treating the journey map as a one-time workshop output never to be updated.
antipattern.reason: Journeys change when products, policies, or channels change. A stale map actively misleads teams by presenting an outdated picture as current reality. Schedule map reviews after major product changes.

antipattern.description: Creating the map in a silo without cross-functional team participation.
antipattern.reason: Journey maps reveal problems that span organizational boundaries. Without the relevant teams present, the map does not produce cross-team ownership of the pain points it surfaces.
